title: The Corruption of Scripture
challenge: Islam's central charge against Christianity is tahrif — the claim that the Bible has been corrupted over centuries, rendering it unreliable. The Quran presents itself as the corrective final revelation, superseding all prior scriptures. If the Torah and Gospel have been altered, then the foundations of Jewish and Christian theology collapse, and Muhammad's corrective mission becomes necessary.
response: This is historically the weakest pillar of Islamic apologetics. The manuscript evidence for the New Testament is overwhelming: over 5,800 Greek manuscripts, with the earliest fragments (P52, P66, P75) dating to within decades of the original writings — predating Islam by five centuries. No serious textual scholar, including non-Christian critics, accepts the corruption thesis. Furthermore, the Quran itself repeatedly affirms the Torah and Gospel as valid and authoritative (Surah 5:47, 5:68, 10:94) — instructing Muhammad to consult 'the People of the Book' if in doubt. If those scriptures were already corrupted at the time of the Quran's composition, this instruction is incoherent. The corruption narrative is a post-hoc theological necessity invented to explain contradictions between the Quran and earlier scripture, not a historically documented event.

title: The Denial of the Crucifixion
challenge: The Quran explicitly states that Jesus was not crucified: 'They did not kill him, nor did they crucify him, but it was made to appear so' (Surah 4:157). Islam teaches that God would not permit his prophet to suffer such a humiliating death, and that another person — possibly Judas — was substituted in his place. The crucifixion narrative is therefore a Christian fabrication or a divine deception designed to test faith.
response: The crucifixion of Jesus under Pontius Pilate is among the most historically certain events of the ancient world — affirmed not only by all four Gospels and Paul's early letters but by non-Christian sources including Tacitus (Annals 15.44), Josephus (Antiquities 18.3), and the Babylonian Talmud. The historian John Dominic Crossan — no friend of Christian orthodoxy — called it 'as sure as anything historical can ever be.' Islam's denial, written 600 years after the event with no cited historical sources, faces an insurmountable evidential burden. Furthermore, the substitution theory creates a theological problem: if God deceived the disciples into believing Jesus died and rose again, then the entire Christian movement was founded on a divinely engineered lie — which raises profound questions about the character of the God Islam claims to worship. The Resurrection, which depends on the Crucifixion, is the cornerstone of Christian apologetics precisely because it is historically verifiable.

title: The Credentials of Muhammad
challenge: Islam rests entirely on the prophetic claim of Muhammad — that he received direct revelation from God through the angel Gabriel over 23 years. Muslims argue that the Quran itself, as an unparalleled literary and spiritual achievement, constitutes the primary miracle validating his prophethood. The rapid expansion of Islam across Arabia and beyond is cited as further evidence of divine sanction.
response: The Christian evaluates prophetic claims against two criteria established in Scripture: doctrinal consistency with prior revelation (Deuteronomy 13:1-5) and verifiable signs. Muhammad's revelations fail the first test — they contradict the established testimony of the Torah and Gospel on central matters including the nature of Jesus, the Crucifixion, and the Trinity. On the miracle question, Muhammad himself acknowledged in multiple hadith that he was not given miracles like prior prophets (Sahih Bukhari 9.92.379). The Quran as literary miracle is a circular argument — its excellence can only be judged by Arabic speakers and presupposes the very tradition it claims to validate. More significantly, the rapid spread of Islam followed military conquest and political consolidation under the early Caliphates, not the pattern of a persecuted minority movement whose founder rose from the dead. By contrast, the early Christian movement spread despite persecution, without political power, driven by eyewitnesses willing to die for what they claimed to have seen.

title: The Trinity vs. Tawhid
challenge: Islam's most fundamental theological objection to Christianity is shirk — the association of partners with God, which Islam considers the one unforgivable sin. The doctrine of the Trinity, in the Islamic reading, claims that God is three gods, which violates pure monotheism (tawhid). The Quran addresses this directly: 'Say not Trinity — desist, it is better for you. Allah is only one God' (Surah 4:171). Christianity is therefore guilty of the gravest possible theological error.
response: Islam's critique of the Trinity attacks a position Christianity does not hold. Tritheism — three gods — has been explicitly condemned as heresy by every major Christian council since Nicaea (325 AD). The Trinity asserts one God in three Persons, not three gods. The distinction is metaphysical, not semantic. Islam's tawhid, taken to its logical conclusion, produces a God who was entirely alone before creation — which raises the question of how love, relationship, and communication can be eternal attributes of a being who had no one to love, relate to, or communicate with until He created the universe. The Christian doctrine of the Trinity resolves this: love, relationship, and self-giving are eternal within the Godhead itself. Furthermore, the Quran's formulation of the Trinity (Surah 5:116) suggests the three persons are the Father, Jesus, and Mary — a version of the Trinity no Christian has ever held, raising questions about the accuracy of the Quranic source material on Christian doctrine. Engaging Islam's critique requires first correcting what Islam thinks the Trinity claims.
